title: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies

items.subtopic: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies | RARE LYMPHOID MALIGNANCIES | MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 1)
items.title: Distinguishing Features of B-Cell Prolymphocytic Leukemia
items.content: B-Cell Prolymphocytic Leukemia (B-PLL) is characterized by medium-sized, round lymphocytes with a prominent nucleolus and light blue cytoplasm. It predominantly affects the blood, bone marrow, and spleen without causing adenopathy. The median age of affected patients is 70 years, with a male-to-female ratio of 1.6.
keyPoints: B-PLL cells express surface IgM (with or without IgD) and typical B-cell markers (CD19, CD20, CD22).
CD23 is absent; about one-third of cases express CD5.
t(11;14) translocation present in 20% of cases.
Mutation or loss of p53 in about half of patients.
Deletions noted in 11q23 and 13q14.
CHOP plus rituximab may be more effective than CHOP alone.

items.subtopic: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies | RARE LYMPHOID MALIGNANCIES | MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 1)
items.title: Clinical Presentation and Prognosis of Splenic Marginal Zone Lymphoma
items.content: Splenic Marginal Zone Lymphoma (SMZL) presents with splenomegaly or incidental lymphocytosis. It is characterized by small lymphocytes originating in the spleen's marginal zone, expressing surface immunoglobulin and CD20 but negative for CD5, CD10, CD43, and CD103.
keyPoints: Median age of patients is mid-fifties; equal representation of men and women.
40% of patients have deletions or translocations involving 7q21 (FLNC gene).
NOTCH2 mutations seen in 25% of patients.
Chromosome 8p deletions may be noted.
Median survival exceeds 10 years; poor prognosis with elevated LDH, anemia, and hypoalbuminemia.

items.subtopic: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies | RARE LYMPHOID MALIGNANCIES | MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 1)
items.title: Characteristics and Treatment of Hairy Cell Leukemia
items.content: Hairy cell leukemia is characterized by small lymphocytes with oval nuclei, abundant cytoplasm, and distinctive membrane projections. It presents with splenomegaly, diffuse bone marrow involvement, and pancytopenia.
keyPoints: Strong expression of CD22, CD25, and CD103; soluble CD25 level in serum is an excellent tumor marker.
Cells express tartrate-resistant acid phosphatase.
Immunoglobulin genes are rearranged and mutated.
Most cases contain the activating BRAF mutation V600E.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies | RARE LYMPHOID MALIGNANCIES | MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 1)

items.title: Vemurafenib and Rituximab for Chemotherapy-Refractory Patients
items.content: In patients with mature B-cell neoplasms refractory to chemotherapy, vemurafenib, a BRAF inhibitor, can be used. While not curative, it helps maintain responses when combined with rituximab.
keyPoints: Vemurafenib is effective for chemotherapy-refractory patients.
Responses are maintained with chronic treatment.
Adding rituximab to vemurafenib results in more durable remissions.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 2)

items.title: Mediastinal Large B-Cell Lymphoma Characteristics and Treatment
items.content: Mediastinal large B-cell lymphoma is distinct from diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, often affecting women more than men with a median age of 35–40 years. Treatments like MACOP-B plus rituximab and EPOCH plus rituximab show high survival rates.
keyPoints: Male-to-female ratio is 1:2–3.
Median age is 35–40 years.
5-year survival with MACOP-B plus rituximab is 75–87%.
EPOCH plus rituximab achieves a 5-year survival of 97%.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 2)

items.title: Intravascular Large B-Cell Lymphoma Diagnosis and Prognosis
items.content: This rare form of large B-cell lymphoma presents with small-vessel occlusion symptoms, skin lesions, or neurologic issues. It is aggressive, often diagnosed late, and poorly responsive to therapy.
keyPoints: Characterized by lymphoma in the lumen of small vessels.
Symptoms include small-vessel occlusion, skin lesions, or neurologic symptoms.
Clinical course is aggressive with poor response to therapy.
Diagnosis often made late or at autopsy.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 2)

items.title: Primary Effusion Lymphoma: Key Features and Management
items.content: Primary effusion lymphoma is a variant of diffuse large B-cell lymphoma presenting with pleural effusions, often without tumor mass lesions. It commonly occurs in immunocompromised individuals, especially those with AIDS, due to HHV-8/KSHV infection. The malignant cells express CD20 and CD79a but may lack immunoglobulin expression. Treatment includes CHOP plus lenalidomide or bortezomib, alongside antiretroviral therapy for HIV.
keyPoints: Occurs in immune deficiency, especially AIDS
Caused by HHV-8/KSHV infection
Cells express CD20 and CD79a; may lack immunoglobulin expression
Rapid progression with death within 6 months if untreated
Treatment: CHOP plus lenalidomide or bortezomib, maintain antiretroviral therapy
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 3)

items.title: Lymphomatoid Granulomatosis: Diagnosis and Treatment
items.content: Lymphomatoid granulomatosis is an angiocentric, angiodestructive lymphoproliferative disease caused by Epstein-Barr virus-infected B cells. It often presents in adults as a pulmonary infiltrate with possible extranodal involvement (kidney 32%, liver 29%, skin 25%, brain 25%). The disease can be remitting and relapsing or rapidly progressive, with the course predicted by histologic grade. Low-grade disease may respond to interferon $ \\$alpha $, while most cases are curable with combination chemotherapy.
keyPoints: Caused by Epstein-Barr virus-infected B cells
Presents as pulmonary infiltrate; extranodal involvement common (kidney 32%, liver 29%, skin 25%, brain 25%)
Course varies: remitting/relapsing or rapidly progressive
Predicted by histologic grade
Treatment: combination chemotherapy, interferon $ \\$alpha $ for low-grade disease
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 3)

items.subtopic: MATURE T-CELL AND NK CELL NEOPLASMS (part 1)
items.title: Diagnosis and Characteristics of T-Cell Polyclonal Leukemia
items.content: T-cell polyclonal leukemia is identified by its aggressive nature, affecting multiple organs including blood, marrow, nodes, liver, spleen, and skin. It presents with a high WBC count often exceeding 100,000/μL, hepatosplenomegaly, and adenopathy in most patients. Skin involvement occurs in about 20%. Diagnosis involves identifying cells on a PB smear that are larger than small lymphocytes, with cytoplasmic blebs and indented nuclei. These cells express T-cell markers such as CD2, CD3, and CD7; two-thirds are CD4+ and CD8-, while 25% are both CD4+ and CD8+. Clonal rearrangement of T-cell receptor β chains is a hallmark, with chromosome 14 inversions occurring in 80% of patients. Additional genetic abnormalities include t(14;14) translocations and deletions in the ATM gene.
keyPoints: Accounts for 1–2% of small lymphocytic leukemias
Elevated WBC count often >100,000/μL
Skin involvement occurs in 20%
Cells express CD2, CD3, CD7; two-thirds are CD4+ and CD8-, 25% are both CD4+ and CD8+
Chromosome 14 inversion in 80%, t(14;14) translocations in 10%
Median survival is about 12 months
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · MATURE T-CELL AND NK CELL NEOPLASMS (part 1)

items.subtopic: MATURE T-CELL AND NK CELL NEOPLASMS (part 1)
items.title: Clinical Features and Management of T-Cell Large Granular Lymphocytic Leukemia
items.content: T-cell large granular lymphocytic leukemia is characterized by increased numbers of LGLs in the peripheral blood, often accompanied by severe neutropenia. It may present with pure red cell aplasia in 15–20% of patients and splenomegaly in 25%. Patients frequently have systemic autoimmune diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis. The disease course is generally indolent, dominated by neutropenia. Treatment paradoxically includes immunosuppressive therapy like cyclosporine or methotrexate to increase granulocyte counts.
keyPoints: LGL count in PB: 2000–20,000/μL
Severe neutropenia often present
Pure red cell aplasia in 15–20%
Splenomegaly in 25% of patients
Systemic autoimmune diseases common
Immunosuppressive therapy can increase granulocyte counts
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · MATURE T-CELL AND NK CELL NEOPLASMS (part 1)

items.subtopic: MATURE T-CELL AND NK CELL NEOPLASMS (part 1)
items.title: Characteristics and Clinical Course of Aggressive NK Cell Leukemia
items.content: Aggressive NK cell leukemia is a rare condition with variable clinical courses, more common in Asians. It often involves clonal Epstein-Barr virus infection. The disease presents with fever, pancytopenia, hepatosplenomegaly, and occasionally hemophagocytosis or multiorgan failure. Cells express CD2 and CD56 but lack rearranged T-cell receptor genes. Chromosome 6 deletions are common. Aggressive forms progress rapidly, while more indolent variants may be discovered incidentally with LGL lymphocytosis.
keyPoints: Rare, more common in Asians
Clonal Epstein-Barr virus infection often present
Symptoms include fever and pancytopenia
Hepatosplenomegaly common, node involvement less so
Cells express CD2 and CD56
Chromosome 6 deletions common
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · MATURE T-CELL AND NK CELL NEOPLASMS (part 1)

items.subtopic: MATURE T-CELL AND NK CELL NEOPLASMS (part 2)
items.title: Risk Factors and Survival in Extranodal NK/T-cell Lymphoma, Nasal Type
items.content: Extranodal NK/T-cell lymphoma, nasal type, has a survival rate linked to the number of defined risk factors. Combination chemotherapy is effective, especially for localized disease, with radiation therapy often following.
keyPoints: Four risk factors: B symptoms, advanced stage, elevated LDH, regional lymph node involvement.
5-year survival rates: 81% (zero risk factors), 64% (one risk factor), 32% (two risk factors), 7% (three or four risk factors).
Combination regimens without anthracyclines may be superior to CHOP, but data are sparse.
High-dose therapy with stem cell transplantation's role is unclear.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · MATURE T-CELL AND NK CELL NEOPLASMS (part 2)

items.subtopic: MATURE T-CELL AND NK CELL NEOPLASMS (part 2)
items.title: Prognosis and Treatment of Enteropathy-Type T-Cell Lymphoma
items.content: Enteropathy-type T-cell lymphoma, associated with celiac disease, has a poor prognosis but can respond to CHOP chemotherapy. Recurrence in the small bowel is possible.
keyPoints: Median survival: 7–11 months.
Tumor expresses CD3 and CD7 nearly always; may express CD8 and CD30.
Most patients have HLA genotype associated with celiac disease (HLA-DQA1*0501 or DQB1*0201).
Bowel perforation can occur in responding patients.
Recurrence may develop elsewhere in the small bowel affected by celiac disease.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · MATURE T-CELL AND NK CELL NEOPLASMS (part 2)

items.subtopic: MATURE T-CELL AND NK CELL NEOPLASMS (part 2)
items.title: Characteristics and Management of Hepatosplenic T-Cell Lymphoma
items.content: Hepatosplenic T-cell lymphoma affects young people with immunodeficiency or autoimmune disease. It has an aggressive course, responding better to cytarabine/etoposide/platinum-based regimens than CHOP.
keyPoints: Derived from gamma/delta T cells.
Common in patients with thiopurine and infliximab history.
Cells are CD3+, usually CD4- and CD8-, may have isochromosome 7q and trisomy 8.
Median survival: about 2 years.
Combination chemotherapy can induce remissions, but most patients relapse.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · MATURE T-CELL AND NK CELL NEOPLASMS (part 2)

items.subtopic: MATURE T-CELL AND NK CELL NEOPLASMS (part 3)
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Primary Cutaneous CD30+ T-Cell Lymphoma
items.content: Primary cutaneous CD30+ T-cell lymphoma, a subset of cutaneous T-cell tumors, is characterized by skin involvement with cells resembling anaplastic T-cell lymphoma. It accounts for about 25% of cutaneous T-cell tumors and can be challenging to distinguish from systemic forms upon dissemination to lymph nodes. The presence of the t(2;5) translocation should prompt consideration of a diagnosis switch to anaplastic T-cell lymphoma. Notably, this lymphoma has been associated with breast implants but generally follows an indolent course.
keyPoints: Approximately 25% of cutaneous T-cell tumors are CD30+ anaplastic lymphomas.
Tumor cells often express CD4 and contain granzyme B and perforin in 70% of cases.
Absence of t(2;5) translocation differentiates it from systemic anaplastic T-cell lymphoma.
Breast implant-associated lymphoma is a rare complication with generally indolent natural history.
Five-year survival exceeds 90%, and treatments include brentuximab vedotin, radiation therapy, and surgery.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · MATURE T-CELL AND NK CELL NEOPLASMS (part 3) | RARE MYELOID MALIGNANCIES

items.subtopic: MATURE T-CELL AND NK CELL NEOPLASMS (part 3)
items.title: Clinical Features and Treatment of Angioimmunoblastic T-Cell Lymphoma
items.content: Angioimmunoblastic T-cell lymphoma is a systemic disease representing about 15% of all T-cell lymphomas. It presents with symptoms such as fever, diffuse adenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, skin rash, and various autoantibodies including cold agglutinins and rheumatoid factor. The nodes show a polymorphous infiltrate with neoplastic T cells and inflammatory cells, along with proliferation of high endothelial venules and follicular dendritic cells. Common chromosomal abnormalities include trisomy 3, trisomy 5, and an extra X chromosome.
keyPoints: ~15% of all T-cell lymphomas are angioimmunoblastic T-cell lymphoma.
Patients often exhibit fever, advanced stage, diffuse adenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, skin rash, polyclonal hypergammaglobulinemia, and autoantibodies.
Common chromosomal abnormalities: trisomy 3, trisomy 5, extra X chromosome.
Aggressive combination chemotherapy can induce regression but may increase infectious complications due to underlying immune defects.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · MATURE T-CELL AND NK CELL NEOPLASMS (part 3) | RARE MYELOID MALIGNANCIES

items.title: Molecular Characterization and Diagnostic Specificity of CNL
items.content: Chronic neutrophilic leukemia is characterized by clonal proliferation of mature neutrophils, primarily due to activating mutations in the CSF3R gene. These mutations are specific to WHO-defined CNL, distinguishing it from other causes of neutrophilia.
keyPoints: CSF3R mutations, particularly C-to-T substitution at nucleotide 1853 (T618I), are found in ~90% of CNL cases.
CSF3R T618I mutation is specific to WHO-defined CNL.
Approximately 40% of T618I-mutated cases also have SETBP1 mutations.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · CHRONIC NEUTROPHILIC LEUKEMIA (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Epidemiology of CNL
items.content: CNL typically presents in elderly patients with a median age at diagnosis of 66.5 years, showing slight male preponderance. It is rare, with an incidence of 0.1 cases/million individuals.
keyPoints: Median age at diagnosis: 66.5 years (range, 15–86 years).
Slight male preponderance in CNL cases (56–58%).
Incidence is approximately 0.1 cases per million individuals.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · CHRONIC NEUTROPHILIC LEUKEMIA (part 1)

items.title: Diagnostic Exclusion Criteria for CNL
items.content: Diagnosis of CNL requires exclusion of other common causes of neutrophilia, such as infections and inflammatory processes, as well as secondary neutrophilia from metastatic cancers or plasma cell neoplasms.
keyPoints: Exclude infections and inflammatory processes when diagnosing CNL.
Consider secondary neutrophilia due to metastatic cancer or plasma cell neoplasms.
Differentiate from other myeloid malignancies, such as AML, which requires ≥10% blasts for diagnosis.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · CHRONIC NEUTROPHILIC LEUKEMIA (part 1)

items.title: CSF3R Mutations and Diagnostic Criteria for CNL
items.content: The diagnosis of Chronic Neutrophilic Leukemia (CNL) can be confirmed by the presence of a membrane proximal CSF3R mutation in patients with predominantly neutrophilic granulocytosis, even without significant leukocytosis. This molecular marker is crucial for distinguishing CNL from other myeloid malignancies.
keyPoints: CSF3R mutations are almost invariably associated with ICC-defined CNL.
Presence of a membrane proximal CSF3R mutation can confirm CNL diagnosis regardless of leukocytosis degree.
Exclusionary criteria must be met in the absence of CSF3R mutations.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · CHRONIC NEUTROPHILIC LEUKEMIA (part 2) | CHRONIC EOSINOPHILIC LEUKEMIA, NOT OTHERWISE SPECIFIED

items.subtopic: CHRONIC EOSINOPHILIC LEUKEMIA, NOT OTHERWISE SPECIFIED
items.title: Characteristics and Treatment Response in CEL-NOS
items.content: Chronic Eosinophilic Leukemia, Not Otherwise Specified (CEL-NOS) is characterized by systemic symptoms, organ involvement, and specific laboratory abnormalities. The median eosinophil count among patients was 6.4 x 10^9/L. Treatment responses vary with hydroxyurea-based regimens showing a persistent decline in eosinophil counts for up to 18 months.
keyPoints: Median eosinophil count: 6.4 x 10^9/L (range, 2.0-53.1 x 10^9/L).
Organ involvement includes spleen, cardiac, pulmonary, and distal esophagus.
88% of patients had cytogenetic abnormalities such as trisomy 8, complex karyotype, 13q-, 20q-, and chromosome 1 abnormalities.
Hydroxyurea-based regimens: median response duration of 18 months.
Prednisone treatment: median response duration of 13 months.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · CHRONIC NEUTROPHILIC LEUKEMIA (part 2) | CHRONIC EOSINOPHILIC LEUKEMIA, NOT OTHERWISE SPECIFIED

items.title: Diagnosis of MPN-U: Recognizing Unclassifiable Myeloproliferative Neoplasms
items.content: MPN-U encompasses myeloproliferative neoplasms that do not fit into established subcategories. Diagnosis is based on the presence of characteristic mutations (e.g., JAK2, CALR) or bone marrow morphology consistent with MPNs, despite normal blood counts and absence of definitive WHO criteria for PV or ET.
keyPoints: MPN-U includes neoplasms that cannot be classified as other MPNs due to atypical presentations such as unusual thrombosis or organomegaly without abnormal blood counts.
Characteristic mutations like JAK2 and CALR, along with bone marrow morphology consistent with MPN, aid in diagnosis despite normal hemoglobin levels or platelet counts.
Some cases of MPN-U may represent early stages of PV or ET that do not meet WHO diagnostic thresholds.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · MYELOPROLIFERATIVE NEOPLASM, UNCLASSIFIABLE

items.title: Management Strategies for MPN-U: Tailoring Treatment to Thrombotic Risk
items.content: Treatment of MPN-U varies based on symptomatology and thrombotic risk. Asymptomatic patients may not require specific interventions, while those with arterial thrombosis might need cytoreductive therapy and aspirin, and those with venous thrombosis should be managed with systemic anticoagulation.
keyPoints: Asymptomatic MPN-U patients typically do not require treatment.
Arterial thrombotic complications in MPN-U may necessitate cytoreductive therapy and aspirin.
Venous thrombosis in MPN-U is managed with systemic anticoagulation.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · MYELOPROLIFERATIVE NEOPLASM, UNCLASSIFIABLE

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria and Initial Workup of CMML
items.content: CMML is diagnosed based on sustained peripheral blood monocytosis, bone marrow morphology, blast count thresholds, and evidence of clonality. It's crucial to exclude reactive causes of monocytosis during the diagnostic workup.
keyPoints: Defined by PB monocytosis ($ \geq0.5 \times 10^9/L $; monocytes $ \geq10\% $) for >3 months
BM morphology consistent with CMML, <20% BM or PB blasts
Exclude reactive causes like infections and connective tissue diseases during workup
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · CHRONIC MYELOMONOCYTIC LEUKEMIA

items.title: Risk Stratification and Treatment in CMML
items.content: The Mayo Molecular Model (MMM) stratifies CMML patients into risk categories based on specific genetic mutations and hematologic parameters, guiding treatment decisions.
keyPoints: Mayo Molecular Model includes ASXL1 truncating mutations, monocyte count >10 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin <10 g/dL, platelet count <100 × 10^9/L
Risk categories: high (3+ factors), intermediate-2 (2 factors), intermediate-1 (1 factor), low (0 factors)
Median survival by risk category: high (16 months), intermediate-2 (31 months), intermediate-1 (59 months), low (97 months)
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · CHRONIC MYELOMONOCYTIC LEUKEMIA

items.title: Treatment Outcomes and Considerations in CMML
items.content: Allogeneic stem cell transplantation (ASCT) is the only curative treatment for high-risk CMML, with drug therapies providing limited disease modification.
keyPoints: ASCT offers potential cure or long-term survival, especially for MMM high/intermediate-2 risk patients
4-year relapse-free and overall survival rates post-ASCT: 27% and 33%, respectively
Drug therapy with hydroxyurea and decitabine shows similar OS in high-risk MP-CMML (23.1 vs 18.4 months)
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · CHRONIC MYELOMONOCYTIC LEUKEMIA

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria and Differentiation of aCML
items.content: aCML is diagnosed based on specific criteria including leukocyte count, dysgranulopoiesis, cytopenia, and absence of certain mutations. It must be differentiated from CML, CNL, and CMML using these criteria.
keyPoints: Leukocyte count ≥13 × 10^9/L
Dysgranulopoiesis present
Cytopenia observed
≥10% immature granulocytes
<20% PB or BM myeloblasts
<10% PB monocytes
<10% PB eosinophilia
Absence of BCR::ABL1, PDGFRA, PDGFRB, FGFR1, JAK2, ABL1, FLT3 mutations
Differential diagnosis includes CML (BCR::ABL1 positive), CNL (CSF3R mutations), and CMML (monocytosis with absolute monocyte count ≥0.5 × 10^9/L)
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · ATYPICAL CHRONIC MYELOID LEUKEMIA

items.title: Prognostic Factors and Treatment Outcomes in aCML
items.content: Prognosis in aCML is influenced by factors such as leukocytosis, transfusion requirement, and immature cells. Conventional chemotherapy is ineffective; however, ASCT shows promise.
keyPoints: Median age at diagnosis: 62 years (WHO study), 70 years (Mayo Clinic)
Splenomegaly in 54% of patients
Red cell transfusion requirement in 65%
Leukemic transformation in 40% (WHO study)
Median survival: 25 months (WHO study), 10.8 months (Mayo Clinic)
Prognostic factors: advanced age, low hemoglobin, TET2 mutations
Conventional chemotherapy largely ineffective
ASCT shows favorable outcomes with majority in complete remission after median follow-up of 55 months
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · ATYPICAL CHRONIC MYELOID LEUKEMIA

items.subtopic: MDS/MPN WITH MUTATED SF3B1 OR WITH RING SIDEROBLASTS, BOTH ASSOCIATED WITH THROMBOCYTOSIS OR NOT OTHERWISE SPECIFIED | JUVENILE MYELOMONOCYTIC LEUKEMIA
items.title: Diagnostic Criteria for MDS/MPN Overlap Syndromes
items.content: MDS/MPN overlap syndromes are characterized by features of both myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS) and myeloproliferative neoplasms (MPN), with specific criteria distinguishing between subtypes based on thrombocytosis, anemia, blast percentages, and genetic mutations.
keyPoints: Thrombocytosis is defined as ≥450 × 10⁹/L for MDS/MPN-T-SF3B1 and MDS/MPN-NOS.
MDS/MPN-T-SF3B1 requires SF3B1 mutation with a variant allele frequency (VAF) >10%.
MDS/MPN-RS-T-NOS includes ≥15% bone marrow ring sideroblasts and absence of SF3B1 mutation.
MDS/MPN-NOS is diagnosed when thrombocytosis or leukocytosis is present but criteria for other MDS/MPN entities are not met.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · MDS/MPN WITH MUTATED SF3B1 OR WITH RING SIDEROBLASTS, BOTH ASSOCIATED WITH THROMBOCYTOSIS OR NOT OTHERWISE SPECIFIED | JUVENILE MYELOMONOCYTIC LEUKEMIA

items.title: Characteristics and Diagnostic Criteria of JMML
items.content: JMML is a clonal disorder in early childhood, characterized by leukocytosis, monocytosis, hepatosplenomegaly, thrombocytopenia, elevated fetal hemoglobin, and hypersensitivity to GM-CSF due to RAS/MAPK signaling mutations.
keyPoints: JMML diagnostic criteria include PB monocyte count ≥ 1 × 10⁹/L, <20% blasts in PB or BM, splenomegaly, and absence of BCR::ABL1.
Mutations in the RAS pathway (PTPN11, NRAS, KRAS, NF1, CBL) are found in ~85% of JMML cases.
Treatment choice is allogeneic stem cell transplantation (ASCT), with a 5-year survival rate of ~50%.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · MDS/MPN WITH MUTATED SF3B1 OR WITH RING SIDEROBLASTS, BOTH ASSOCIATED WITH THROMBOCYTOSIS OR NOT OTHERWISE SPECIFIED | JUVENILE MYELOMONOCYTIC LEUKEMIA

items.title: Transient Myeloproliferative Disorder in Down Syndrome
items.content: TMD, or transient abnormal myelopoiesis (TAM), is an abnormal megakaryoblast proliferation seen in approximately 10% of infants with Down syndrome. It can spontaneously regress in about 75% of cases or progress to acute megakaryoblastic leukemia (AMKL) in the remaining 25%. Almost all TMD and TMD-derived AMKL cases involve somatic GATA1 mutations.
keyPoints: Occurs in ~10% of infants with Down syndrome
Spontaneous regression occurs in 75% of cases
Progresses to acute megakaryoblastic leukemia (AMKL) in 25% of cases
Almost all patients display somatic GATA1 mutations
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · TRANSIENT MYELOPROLIFERATIVE DISORDER | HYPEREOSINOPHILIA

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria for Hypereosinophilia
items.content: Hypereosinophilia (HE) is defined by a peripheral blood absolute eosinophil count (AEC) of ≥1500 × 10^9/L. Diagnostic evaluation includes bone marrow and peripheral blood examination, with specific tests like cytogenetic analysis, immunohistochemistry for mast cells, lymphocyte flow cytometry, TCR gene rearrangement studies, and serum tryptase.
keyPoints: HE defined by AEC ≥1500 × 10^9/L
Bone marrow examination includes cytogenetic and molecular analysis
Peripheral blood examination includes lymphocyte flow cytometry and TCR gene rearrangement
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · TRANSIENT MYELOPROLIFERATIVE DISORDER | HYPEREOSINOPHILIA

items.title: Primary Hypereosinophilia Diagnostic Approach
items.content: In cases of HE with normal bone marrow morphology and no genetic abnormalities, consider lymphocytic variant HE or idiopathic HE. If tissue/organ dysfunction is present due to eosinophilic infiltrates, the conditions are termed hypereosinophilic syndromes (HES). Exclude myeloid/lymphoid neoplasms with eosinophilia and tyrosine kinase fusion genes (TKGF) before considering other causes.
keyPoints: Lymphocytic variant HE or idiopathic HE considered if normal BM morphology
Presence of tissue dysfunction modifies diagnosis to hypereosinophilic syndrome
Exclude myeloid/lymphoid neoplasms with eosinophilia and TKGF
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · TRANSIENT MYELOPROLIFERATIVE DISORDER | HYPEREOSINOPHILIA

items.subtopic: HYPEREOSINOPHILIA ASSOCIATED WITH TYROSINE KINASE GENE FUSIONS
items.title: FIP1L1-PDGFRA Fusion Gene and Imatinib Response
items.content: The FIP1L1-PDGFRA fusion gene, resulting from an ~800 kb interstitial deletion within chromosome 4q12, leads to a constitutively active tyrosine kinase. This mutation is associated with hyper eosinophilia and systemic mastocytosis or hypereosinophilic syndrome (HES) phenotypes. Imatinib therapy reliably induces complete hematologic and molecular responses in patients harboring this fusion gene.
keyPoints: FIP1L1-PDGFRA fusion results from an ~800 kb interstitial deletion within 4q12.
Occurs in a subset of patients with systemic mastocytosis or HES phenotypes.
Imatinib therapy leads to complete hematologic and molecular responses in all treated patients (148 out of 148).
1-, 5-, and 10-year overall survival rates for imatinib-treated patients are 99%, 95%, and 84% respectively.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · HYPEREOSINOPHILIA ASSOCIATED WITH TYROSINE KINASE GENE FUSIONS

items.subtopic: HYPEREOSINOPHILIA ASSOCIATED WITH TYROSINE KINASE GENE FUSIONS
items.title: PDGFRB Rearrangement in Myeloid Malignancies
items.content: The PDGFRB rearrangement, first characterized by the ETV6-PDGFRB fusion (t(5;12)(q33;p13)), leads to constitutive activation of PDGFRB signaling. This mutation is associated with eosinophilic myeloid malignancies and responds well to imatinib therapy.
keyPoints: ETV6-PDGFRB fusion involves the tyrosine kinase encoding region of PDGFRB fused to ETV6.
Results in constitutive activation of PDGFRB signaling.
Imatinib therapy is effective for treating these myeloid malignancies.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · HYPEREOSINOPHILIA ASSOCIATED WITH TYROSINE KINASE GENE FUSIONS

items.subtopic: HYPEREOSINOPHILIA ASSOCIATED WITH TYROSINE KINASE GENE FUSIONS
items.title: 8p11 Myeloproliferative Syndrome and FGFR1 Rearrangements
items.content: The 8p11 myeloproliferative syndrome (EMS) is characterized by FGFR1 fusion mutations, such as ZMYM2::FGFR1. This leads to a disease phenotype with features of both lymphoma and eosinophilic MPN. EMS requires aggressive treatment, often involving combination chemotherapy followed by autologous stem cell transplantation (ASCT). Pemi-gib is effective in inducing responses in >70% of patients.
keyPoints: EMS involves FGFR1 fusion mutations like ZMYM2::FGFR1, t(8;13)(p11.1;q12.1).
Disease features include T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia, large B-cell lymphoma, and MPN-like disease.
Treatment often requires combination chemotherapy followed by ASCT.
Pemi-gib induces hematologic and cytogenetic responses in >70% of patients with FGFR1-rearranged neoplasms.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · HYPEREOSINOPHILIA ASSOCIATED WITH TYROSINE KINASE GENE FUSIONS

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria and Initial Evaluation of HES
items.content: Hyper eosinophilic syndrome (HES) is characterized by a persistent increase in absolute eosinophil count (AEC) to ≥1.5 × 10^9/L with evidence of organ damage mediated by eosinophils. Before diagnosing HES, bone marrow studies and additional blood tests such as serum tryptase, T-cell immunophenotyping, and T-cell receptor gene rearrangement analysis are recommended.
keyPoints: HES is a subtype of idiopathic eosinophilia with AEC ≥1.5 × 10^9/L.
Organ damage includes cardiomyopathy, gastroenteritis, cutaneous lesions, sinusitis, pneumonitis, neuritis, and vasculitis.
Initial evaluation should include echocardiogram and serum troponin to screen for myocardial involvement.
Increased serum cardiac troponin correlates with cardiomyopathy in HES.
Echocardiographic findings may show ventricular apical thrombus, valve abnormalities, endocardial thickening, dilated left ventricle, or pericardial effusion.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · HYPEREOSINOPHILIC SYNDROME

items.title: Management and Treatment Options for HES
items.content: Glucocorticoids are the primary treatment for HES. Oral prednisone is initiated at 1 mg/kg per day, tapered over 2–3 months. For symptoms persisting at doses >10 mg/d, hydroxyurea or interferon α may be used as steroid-sparing agents. Mepolizumab and alemtuzumab are considered for refractory cases.
keyPoints: Glucocorticoids (prednisone) are the cornerstone of HES therapy.
Prednisone is started at 1 mg/kg per day, tapered over 2–3 months.
Steroid-sparing agents include hydroxyurea or interferon α for symptoms >10 mg/d.
Mepolizumab (IL-5 targeting) reduces disease flare-ups significantly; FDA approved on September 25, 2020.
Alemtuzumab targets CD52 and may be considered in refractory cases.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · HYPEREOSINOPHILIC SYNDROME

items.title: Classification and Diagnostic Criteria of Systemic Mastocytosis
items.content: Systemic mastocytosis (SM) is classified into various subtypes based on the presence of neoplastic mast cells in bone marrow or extracutaneous organs. The ICC and WHO5 systems provide criteria for diagnosis, distinguishing between indolent, smoldering, aggressive forms, and those associated with myeloid or hematologic neoplasms.
keyPoints: SM is subclassified into ISM, SSM, ASM, SM-AMN (ICC), SM-AHN (WHO5), and MCL.

Diagnosis per ICC requires a major criterion (multifocal aggregates of ≥15 MCs) or three minor criteria: BM biopsy with >25% atypical MCs, MC expression of CD25/CD2/CD30, KIT D816V mutation, increased serum tryptase >20 ng/mL.
WHO5 diagnosis requires a major criterion plus one other minor criterion or three minor criteria.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · MASTOCYTOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Distinguishing Advanced Systemic Mastocytosis
items.content: Advanced systemic mastocytosis (AdvSM) includes aggressive SM, SM with associated myeloid or hematologic neoplasm, and mast cell leukemia. It is distinguished by the presence of an associated neoplasm or organopathy due to MC infiltration.
keyPoints: AdvSM includes ASM, SM-AMN/AHN, and MCL.
MC-associated organopathy defined by ≥1 cytopenia, palpable hepatosplenomegaly with abnormal function tests, gastrointestinal malabsorption, or large osteolytic lesions.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · MASTOCYTOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Prognostic Differences in Advanced Systemic Mastocytosis Classifications
items.content: The ICC and WHO5 classifications differ in their prognostic implications for advanced systemic mastocytosis, particularly regarding mast cell leukemia and associated neoplasms.
keyPoints: ICC-defined MCL has worse survival compared to WHO5-defined MCL with mature MC cytomorphology.
Prognostic distinction exists between ICC-defined MCL and SM-AMN but not WHO5-defined MCL versus SM-AHN.
ICC-defined SM-AMN is prognostically distinct from ASM, unlike WHO5-defined SM-AHN with lymphoid lineage.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · MASTOCYTOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Treatment Prioritization in SM-AMN
items.content: In systemic mastocytosis with an associated myeloid neoplasm (SM-AMN), the treatment approach prioritizes based on the severity of the acute myeloid neoplasm (AMN) component. High-grade AMNs, such as AML or high/very-high-risk MDS with >10% bone marrow blasts, take precedence in decision-making.
keyPoints: High-grade AMNs like AML and high-/very-high-risk MDS (>10% BM blasts) should be prioritized for treatment.
Intensive remission induction or hypomethylating agent therapy is considered for fit patients with SM-AML or SM-high/very-high-risk MDS.
Supportive care, including antihistamines and anaphylaxis precautions, is recommended if the SM component is minimal.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · MASTOCYTOSIS (part 2)

items.title: Role of ASCT in SM-AMN
items.content: Autologous stem cell transplantation (ASCT) plays a crucial role in treating SM with high-grade AMNs. It is most effective when preceded by cytoreduction, particularly using myeloablative conditioning.
keyPoints: ASCT is vital for SM-high-grade AMN treatment.
Survival is superior with myeloablative versus reduced-intensity conditioning prior to ASCT.
Effective cytoreduction before ASCT improves overall survival outcomes.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · MASTOCYTOSIS (part 2)

items.title: Understanding the Classification and Prognosis of Histiocytic Sarcoma
items.content: Histiocytic sarcoma is a rare malignant proliferation of mature tissue histiocytes, often presenting with localized disease. It can be associated with systemic symptoms like fever and weight loss, and involves sites such as lymph nodes, skin, and the gastrointestinal system. The prognosis is generally poor due to ineffective treatment options.
keyPoints: Histiocytic sarcoma represents malignant proliferation of mature tissue histiocytes.
Median age at diagnosis: 46 years; slight male predilection.
Common sites include lymph nodes, skin, and gastrointestinal system.
Prognosis is poor with median survival estimated at 2 years for malignant histiocytosis.
Factors associated with poor outcome: older age, high comorbidity index, disease involving the hematopoietic and reticuloendothelial system.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · DENDRITIC AND HISTIOCYTIC NEOPLASMS (part 1)

items.title: Characteristics and Management of Langerhans Cell Histiocytosis
items.content: Langerhans cell histiocytosis is a neoplastic proliferation of specialized dendritic cells, primarily affecting children with a male predilection. It can present as unifocal or multifocal disease, with prognosis depending on the organs involved. Treatment varies based on disease extent and may include targeted therapies for high-risk mutations.
keyPoints: LCH incidence: 5 per million; typically affects children with male predilection.
Unifocal (eosinophilic granuloma) vs. multifocal presentations.
Approximately 55% of patients harbor BRAF V600E mutations, indicating high-risk disease.
Treatment includes local therapies for unifocal disease and systemic chemotherapy or targeted therapy for multisystem involvement.
Overall response rate with cladribine: 81%; ten-year progression-free survival: 90.9%.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · DENDRITIC AND HISTIOCYTIC NEOPLASMS (part 1)

items.title: Targeted Therapy in Non-LCH Dendritic and Histiocytic Neoplasms
items.content: In non-Langerhans cell histiocytosis, such as Erdheim-Chester disease and Rosai-Dorfman disease, targeted therapy with MEK inhibitors like trametinib shows promise. The response rate is notable even in patients without BRAF V600E mutations, highlighting the role of MAPK pathway alterations.
keyPoints: Trametinib used for non-LCH diseases including Erdheim-Chester disease and Rosai-Dorfman disease.
Common treatment-related toxicity: rash (27%).
Response rate in evaluable patients: 71%, with 73% without detectable BRAF V600E.
Median time-to-treatment failure: 37 months.
Most patients have mutations in the MAPK pathway, including BRAF and other genes like MAP2K, NF1, GNAS, or RAS.
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · DENDRITIC AND HISTIOCYTIC NEOPLASMS (part 1)

items.title: Langerhans Cell Sarcoma: Prognosis and Presentation
items.content: Langerhans cell sarcoma (LCS) is a neoplastic proliferation of Langerhans cells with malignant morphology. It can present de novo or progress from antecedent LCH, affecting primarily females with a median age at diagnosis of 41 years. Common sites include the liver, spleen, lung, and bone.
keyPoints: Female predilection
Median age at diagnosis: 41 years
Common sites: liver, spleen, lung, bone
Prognosis is poor with generally ineffective treatment
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · DENDRITIC AND HISTIOCYTIC NEOPLASMS (part 2)

items.title: Interdigitating Dendritic Cell Sarcoma: Clinical Features and Prognosis
items.content: Interdigitating dendritic cell sarcoma (IDCS) is a rare neoplasm affecting elderly adults with no sex predilection. It typically presents as asymptomatic solitary lymphadenopathy. Immunophenotype includes S100+ and negative for vimentin and CD1a.
keyPoints: Extremely rare
Affects elderly adults, no sex predilection
Typical presentation: asymptomatic solitary lymphadenopathy
Immunophenotype: S100+, negative for vimentin and CD1a
Prognosis ranges from benign to widespread lethal disease
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · DENDRITIC AND HISTIOCYTIC NEOPLASMS (part 2)

items.title: Follicular Dendritic Cell Sarcoma: Associations and Treatment
items.content: Follicular dendritic cell sarcomas (FDCNs) are usually localized, affecting adults. They may be associated with Castleman's disease in 10–20% of cases and have an increased incidence in schizophrenia. Common sites include cervical lymph nodes, maxillary, mediastinal, and retroperitoneal lymph nodes.
keyPoints: Usually localized
Possible association with Castleman's disease (10-20%)
Increased incidence reported in schizophrenia
Common sites: cervical, maxillary, mediastinal, retroperitoneal lymph nodes; oral cavity; gastrointestinal system; skin; breast
Immunophenotype includes CD21, CD35, and CD23
Treatment involves surgical excision, regional radiotherapy, sometimes systemic chemotherapy
items.reference: Less Common Lymphoid and Myeloid Malignancies · DENDRITIC AND HISTIOCYTIC NEOPLASMS (part 2)
